The song "Vogue" is one of Madonna's biggest international hits, topping the charts in over 30 countries. It became the world's best-selling single in 1990. In the song, Madonna references sixteen "Golden Age" Hollywood stars from the 1920s to the 1950s. In order of mention in the lyrics, they are Greta Garbo, Marilyn Monroe, Marlene Dietrich, Joe DiMaggio, Marlon Brando, Jimmy Dean, Grace Kelly, Jean Harlow, Gene Kelly, Fred Astaire, Ginger Rogers, Rita Hayworth, Lauren Bacall, Katharine Hepburn, Lana Turner and Bette Davis.

content:
This song was the official start of the '90s decade, released March 27, 1990, a watershed moment in American popular music as nothing would ever be quite the same ever again. All that came before it suddenly became part of the past. BV (before Vogue) and AV (after Vogue) is how it defined the music scene, not just in the US, but around the world. As such, it very much deserves to be among the greatest American popular music milestones of the XXth Century.

I would also add that the song itself represents the continuing legacy of how minorities continue to shape the poplar culture in this country. In this case, African-American and Gay cultures. In fact, the intense subversive vibe the song carries has elevated it to the level of an anthem of defiance and empowerment.

content: I love YouTube

I’ve been listening to Madonna since Ray of Light, so I didn’t actually live through her most controversial era. I was just a kid alone (10 - 9 yo) in my room with an old scratched CD in my portable player, not even understanding the lyrics. And yet I always loved what she did. So I really doubt it’s just a “generation” thing.

I’m French, and even without fully understanding the words at first, I understood very early on the themes and the attitude. I’m a 90s kid. Later I became a huge fan of No Doubt "it's my life" and made the connection especially seeing how Gwen Stefani referenced that era in her own way when I was still really young.

We shouldn’t generalize. To me, Madonna is an "icon". I wouldn’t call her a goddess, but she’s deeply admirable when you consider everything she’s been through, like many other women in the industry. I’m incredibly impressed by her journey. That said, we don’t need to "over-romanticize" it either.

There’s also feminism and female empowerment through music and the power of shock as a tool. That didn’t stop with her. Just look at artists like M.I.A., who have used pop culture to denounce political issues, question power structures, and provoke conversations. Women have continuously used music as a platform to challenge norms and confront society.

All those performances by artists like Madonna, Britney, Alicia Keys… women who endured so much, lived under intense scrutiny, and still delivered powerful voices and spectacular shows. It’s not generational: it’s about impact, courage, and what you choose to stand for!

content:
​ @FindingMadonna  this song was on Erotica album, and you know well the situation surrounding the album's release, which included the notorious book (which now you can find its e-book pdf version for free on the internet 😂). And what happened then, the controversy and all that (remember, public hadnt really been calm and really forgiven her after the Like A Prayer controversy😂) really overshadowed the singles released from this album. 

As almost everybody and press and so on and so on were busy critizing/attacking her for that book, the album and singles were impacted. Some, or many, refused to play them on the radio.
Thats why this and other singles/songs from that album were not much "played" and "heard" compared to the preceding album and its hits singles such as Like A Prayer, Cherish, Vogue.

So although the Erotica album did contain some of Madonna's excellent works, they were not that recognized by mass audience.

And yes, youre right, many people thought her career was over after the controversy, but then there was  "A League of their Own' and 'With Honors' in which she had two soundtrack hits - This used to be my Playground and I'll Remember respectively.

Then 1994 saw the release of her next studio album, Bedtime Story, which featured her longest #1 hit, Take a Bow, and other singles.

By that time i think people had already forgiven her😂

content: Most of the people don't have an idea what singing is all about. What will you do with a great range if your voice sounds bad, or has no musicality to it? Musicality or melodiousness do not come with a great range. Mariah has a great range, but so what? Is her voice as saccharine as Madonna's? Even in her prime, she never sounded as sweet as Madonna does even now. Vocal gymnastics is an altogether separate thing. You might be a master at vocal gymnastics, but that doesn't make your voice sound sweet or melodious. Great range and sweetness of voice is a rare combo and only once in a while we get Whitney Houstons to prove that. I don't get this fixation over range and octaves really. Michael Jackson had a great range, but was his voice anywhere near to the voice of John Denver or Frank Sinatra in sweetness or musicality? Most of the time Michael shouted and screamed with illegible lyrics in his uptempo songs. I'm a fan of Michael, but what is truth is truth. ❤️

Depends on the station.

all stations have a target demographic.
BBC Radio 2 in the uk played Madonna's Ghosttown as one of its a-list songs (daily play through all non specialised shows) but a lot of the other songs from the album are not in the demographic for the station.  
BBC Radio 2 is the most listened to radio station in the uk and attracts a huge range of listeners (all ages, it's one of the few stations that crosses generations).  BBC Radio 2 tend to like promoting either new artists or the ones who will do a live session on either jo wiley's show (which gives heavy promotion on the day, gets repeated several times and gets put on the BBC iplayer for a month) or a unplugged style session on the Friday breakfast show.  A lot of globally famous 'superstars' refuse to do this type of stuff as it's low/no pay, hence why they don't get the airplay.
  
Then there is cost.  Every time a song is played the station has to pay royalties.  A lot of smaller stations can't afford to play the newer releases non stop.  Royalties decrease with age of the song.

Also some artists are more popular in one country than another.  Gwent Stefani/No doubt didn't promote a lot of their stuff in the uk, Return to Saturn wasn't actually released here, I had to import the CD at the time. Only Gwen Stefani's first solo album has been promoted in the uk, that cross advertising not just airplay.

Those who know will remember what an EVENT it was every time Madonna released a new album. What would the videos be like? What would her new look be? The TV interviews, the references to her from other areas of the entertainment and political world. Her LGBTQ+ advocacy back when it wasn't well supported. She influenced so much outside music, she was an industry unto herself and all this, of course, was before social media. 

No matter what else might be said about her or her career, she has been a major influence on society and she has brought a lot of enjoyment and fun to millions of people throughout her career. Personally, to this day, one of her upbeat songs can lift my mood, and I appreciate that.

Most who teach this take the description of the King of Tyrus in Ezekiel 28:12-15 to be a description of Satan. He is said there to have been in the Garden of Eden and was perfect in all his ways until iniquity was found in him. That fits none other but the devil. Therefore, the prince of Tyrus mentioned earlier in the chapter would be the earthly ruler of Tyre, while the king of Tyrus would be the devil who rules over the prince of Tyrus. Daniel, chapter ten, also deals with the connection between many earthly rulers and evil spiritual principalities.

Therefore, taking this to be Satan, Ezekiel 28:13 has a phrase that is very interesting: "the workmanship of thy tabrets and of thy pipes was prepared in thee in the day that thou wast created." That is, he was created with tabrets and pipes built in him. What would this refer to?  Tabrets (spelled taborets in modern English) are small drums like those used to beat out timing for a fife player. Pipes probably refer to tubes used to produce tones by blowing air through them, as in an organ. This shows us that the devil had the makings of percussion instruments and wind instruments built into his very being.

We can also arrive at a good conclusion as to why he had this apparatus built in him. In Ezekiel 28:14, he is called the "anointed cherub that covereth." In Ezekiel 10:1-22, there are four cherubim who stand at the four corners of the throne of God. Since "the glory of the LORD went up from the cherub" (Ezekiel 10:4), the cherubim were there to give glory and praise to the Lord. But here we see no cherub covering the throne.  That had been the job of Satan as the anointed cherub that covered. He would be the fifth cherub and his job would have been to glorify God. It is interesting that the most powerful of God's heavenly creatures is the one who most prominently gives glory and praise to God. In one sense therefore, Satan was the heavenly choir director and he led the songs of praise to the Lord that still ring in heaven. The inbuilt tabrets and pipes would definitely come in handy here.

Now, when Satan fell in rebellion against God, he did not lose the natural abilities that God had given him. Therefore, he kept the tabrets and the pipes. But now, he did not use them to bring glory to the Lord but to turn God's creatures against their Creator. His expertise is seen in the powerful influence he welds today in music. He knows his music and he hates the Lord. This is a dangerous combination.

David Reagan

content: Me

@Cutting Yoko’s bra! Actually, the media said she couldn't sing.  One reviewer of her music said her voice sounded like "Mickey Mouse on helium."  So, no she was not overrated.  She was thought of as fad that would pass and her music was just thought of as bubble gum pop.  But, along with time, she had voice coaches to help her and that's why she finally won the role of the central figure in "Evita."  And it wasn't until she did "Ray of Light" that the critics finally came around to her music.

As far as her image goes, yes, Debbie Harry was definitely an influence.  Also, if you look back, in the 1970s, the group The Runaways were popular for songs like "Cherry Bomb."  The lead singer of the group's most well known costume comprised of her wearing sexy underwear (a bustier, garter belt and stockings).  Plus, there was an underground band (can't remember their name) that wore underwear as outerwear in the very early 1980s. So I think Madonna was definitely more influenced by them than the blonde from ABBA, even though she was a fan of them.  And Kim Wilde was famous at the same time as Madonna.  So I don't think she stole anything from her.  

Also, Madonna bought a lot of her clothes from thrift stores because she didn't have much money back then.  She even has admitted to taking ripped pantyhose, cutting them up and tying them on her hair as a hair band.  So some of her ideas were her own.🤷‍♀️

But, yes, her 80s music was her best.  My favorite album of hers will always be her self titled album "Madonna."🙂
She still is.

By the early 1990s people began to mistrust each other and appeared to be looking for political disagreement.  Political correctness began to bring out the worst in everyone. In 1998 was all about Titanic Celine Dion song, Madonna (this album sold over 18 million copies!), Janet Jackson ("Together Again" was the biggest hit of her carreer) and a couple more things (but not much more global).
Madonna never went anywhere since she started having TOP 10 Hits in 1984 and during 1998 she had another 2 TOP 10 Hits with "Frozen" (#02) and "Ray of Light" (#05). But in 1997 she had a TOP 10 with "Don't Cry For Me Argentina", in 1996 with "You'll See", in 1995 with "Take a Bow" (#01 for 7 weeks), 1994 with "I''ll Remember" (#02) and "Secret"(#03).... and in almost all years before since she started her carreer.

Yes, along with music by Queen and other excellent bands and singer-songwriters, many of whom are from the 80s (when I was a teenager), although I enjoy music from decades since then such as Robyn, Swedish singer-songwriter as well as being a DJ and a record producer and making great songs since 1989. Many artists have covered Robyn's songs such as the band "Kings Of Leon" for the song titled "Dancing On My Own" and people incorrectly assume the band originated that song whilst in reality Robyn was the original songwriter and singer of that song. She's an excellent singer-songwriter and I wish more people knew of her music. Another good song by Robyn is "Call Your Girlfriend." Then there's a band that disbanded about 3-5 years ago now but which is called UHH, short for "Uh Huh Her". UHH has two (2) women singer-songwriters and keyboardists and I enjoy many of their songs, particularly "Not A Love Song" and "Same High" (which some got to know from the soundtrack of the 2010 romantic comedy drama film titled 'The Kids Are Alright'.

content:
@stevencoardvenice  I am a (proud) Boomer because our music really was revolutionary… and actually made via talent and musical instruments. 
I call this Madonna song, techno. Because that is computerized sounds, and synthetic drum beats laid down in the produced track.  Sure, on a recording, it was great dance music in the clubs. 
It was designed for that.
But, how in the heck did she do this in a live concert?  She would have to karaoke to a sound track - which is rather false and UN musical, IMHO. 
Back in the day, When we saw C,S, N, &Y or Led Zep, or the Allman Brothers, or Santana (do all those percussions, especially) it was really their singing and their talented playing of the amplified instruments.  
I highly recommend that everyone watch the old Woodstock movie just to see the very young Santana group en-trance a half a million people with their live, hand crafted music!  As I said, ‘revolutionary’. 
But of course I realize different music suits different purposes at different times.

StefanDot ''Madonna has been successfully sued for plagiarism at least twice — for the song ‘Frozen’ and for the imagery in the video to ‘Hollywood’. And there are numerous other instances where she is clearly plagiarizing the look and/or music of any number of iconic predecessors and contemporaries such as Debbie Harry, Annie Lennox, Cyndi Lauper, etc. All she had going for her musically was a keen ear for emerging trends — she was never a trendsetter herself — that would take real talent and guts. Now that she’s well past her prime, she is deluding herself that she can still capture and capitalize on the next big thing. Or, perhaps she’s kidding herself that she has the artistic weight of other truly talented older acts.

Frozen is banned in Belgium because the courts ruled plagiarism on Madonna’s part. A Belgian songwriter, Salvatore Acquaviva, has won the plagiarism case against Madonna over her hit.

Madonna had to pay the estate of photographer Guy Bourdin in a federal lawsuit in which she plagiarized his work for her “Hollywood” video. She settled for an undisclosed sum, to avoid negative publicity, of course. Want more? Fairuz has a very famous hymn “Al Yawm Ulliqa Alal Khashaba”, released as part of the 1962 album “Good Friday: Eastern Sacred Songs”. The hymn is regularly played on TV in time for Good Friday every year.

Madonna sampled the hymn in 2 songs without permission: The Beast Within (1990) and Erotica (1992). Fairuz sued Madonna for using the hymn in Erotica, where Madonna sang “all over me” while Fairuz chanted “today he is held”. The case never reached court & an undisclosed settlement was reached between the two, while the Erotica single & album were banned in Lebanon and other places. It’s weird that Fairuz didn’t sue Madonna for using the hymn in The Beast Within and instead sued her for Erotica, even though the former was released 2 years prior.

Madonna also paid poet Max Blagg for ripping off lines from a poem of his for her song Sky Fits Heaven. Madonna has been stealing ideas from young up and commers to make herself seem hip and relevant for years. So she doesn’t have a lot of room to talk.

Not to mention that in fact, Madonna has some of the most ignorant fans. When you put celebrities on pedestals, you become slaves to surface image, not facts & reality. Madonna has become a parody of her former self clinging to whoever is “relevant” (Miley Cyrus, Lady Gaga, Katy Perry) just to try get more attention. If only she would put this much effort into her music which has become god awful now.

Lady Gaga isn’t the only person Madonna attacks on her upcoming 13th album. Her ex-husband Guy Ritchie also getting a dressing down on her song Heartbreak City. She accuses the Layer Cake director of only being with her for the fame.

“Cut me down the middle, f***ed me up a little. You said I was your queen. I tried to give you everything. And now you want your freedom,” she sings. “You got just what you came for, a bit of fame and fortune, and I’m no longer needed. You tore me into pieces, you didn’t have no damn reason. I let you in my kingdom, you helped yourself with everything”, she goes on. “I curse the day we met. This memory is haunting me. I wish I could forget”.

Is this a joke? Is this considered real music? Madonna and her ego need to retire. She doesn’t need relevancy any longer, she has a legacy that will live on even after she is no longer on earth.'' Source: Glammonitor.com

Buck Naked Whitney’s most recent single “Higher Love” was just a remix of an unreleased track and hit #2 in the UK, charted in the Billboard Hot 100, and has over 230 million streams on  Spotify, which is more successful than any song that Madonna has released in ages.

And how does Whitney have fewer pop classics when she’s released the highest selling female single of all time (I Will Always Love You), I Wanna Dance With Somebody (which is the most streamed track by any female artist from the 80s) and whose songs like I Have Nothing, Greatest Love of All, I’m Every Woman and Saving All My Love For You are still the most covered songs on talent shows globally (and have been for the past 20 years, long before she passed away).

She’s also still the most awarded female artist in music history and has continued to have a level of respect for her artistry despite her personal demons.

She’s the most streamed woman of her generation because her music, voice, natural beauty (she was a model before she was a singer) and talent speaks for itself. Her back catalogue of music was still selling very well prior to her death and her last studio album went #1.

MJ’s music videos are highly watched 10 years after his death because they’re really good — creative, high quality, great music and dancing talent that’s unmatched by anyone except Janet. To reduce his music video legacy to 3 or so videos is ridiculous when more of the world’s population and general public sees him as the most iconic music video artist of all time. The VMAs named the lifetime achievement award the MICHAEL JACKSON VIDEO VANGUARD award for a reason...

With 14 studio , 3 soundtrack , 5  live , 6 compilation , and a slew of the most famous singles and music videos of all time, she's been omnipresent in pop for over three decades. Out of the top eight best-selling musicians of all time, she's the only female. She's arguably the first female performer to ever have complete control over every aspect of her image, paving the way for every one of today's liberated female pop acts. Madonna did all she could to push artistic boundaries and challenge audience perceptions. She said "fuck" on national television, experimented with erotic and religious choreography — often at the same time — and pushed almost every sexual taboo she could think of.

That's something she's still doing to this day. She's taking aim at our culture's ageism. 

Women, generally, when they reach a certain age, have accepted that they're not allowed to behave a certain way. But She  didn't  follow the rules. She never did,  So She  have to be the first person who opens the door for women to believe and understand and embrace the idea that they can be sexual and look good and be as relevant in their 50s or their 60s or whatever as they were in their 20s, then so be it." Empowering women artist , she started it all. She brought yoga to the masses. Listen, did yoga really exist before Madonna? Nope!  - She became pop’s headmistress of reinvention. Madonna’s ability to transform herself for albums, videos, photo shoots, tours and movies made her a master of perpetual transformation. As she rolled from one aesthetic to the next, switching up her hair, her choreography, her accent, it became shorthand to describe what was happening in one word: reinvention. She made innerwear outerwear. In “Desperately Seeking Susan,” Madonna reinvented the bra and panties (as a bikini), and stalked a Port Authority Bus Terminal bathroom in her bra. She played an exotic dancer in her “Open Your Heart” video, wearing a black corset with sparkly gold pasties on top. And who could forget the missile-like cone bra of Blond Ambition, which helped establish that in Madonna’s work, sex was less often a thing to be celebrated in and of itself than it was an extra avenue for power. She helped transform Marilyn Monroe from a symbol of victimhood, vulnerable to a spokeswoman for unapologetic sexuality. In 1985, Madonna channeled Marilyn Monroe circa “Gentlemen Prefer Blondes” in her “Material Girl” video. Monroe at that point was in many ways a tragic figure, a woman whose death from barbiturates cemented her status as the sad, sexy girl. That wasn’t Madonna’s interest. With men dancing around her, begging for affection, Madonna made her tribute a statement of sexual assertiveness and power. At the same time MADONNA separate herself from Marilyn Monroe video, being a strong clever woman , she owned it.  She was a pioneering ally to L.G.B.T. people everywhere. These days, there’s barely a female pop star alive who doesn’t loudly broadcast her unwavering, if sometimes exploitative, support for L.G.B.T. rights. But in 1991, when Madonna gave her no-holds-barred interview to The Advocate. She was the first great identity artist. Because Madonna treated the genders and other people’s identities as fashion, she made those identities seem fashionable.

Madonna affected the Fashio Industry.
Madonna's cultural influence has no rival.
Madonna pioneered a unique signature look that is copied and recognized today. 
Madonna's fashion sense, attitude, and lifestyle.
Her fashion impacted the music industry, the streets and fashion catwalks.
Heavy makeup, fishnet tights, fingerless lace gloves, religious jewelry, layered necklaces, lace, rubber bracelets define the 80s era's fashion.
The  White Bride outfit is copied and widely recognized nowadays. 
She also launched the trend of underwear as outerwear still prevalent today, seen on celebrities and in street fashions alike.
Corsets, cone bra, fishnets and the ponytail in the 90s has continued to echo through pop culture.
Madonna birthed a major fashion moment with the cone bra.
The high heels over the knee boots, fingerless leather gloves, kimonos, leotards,  in the 00s.
Jean Paul Gaultier and Dolce & Gabanna became Fashion power houses respectively after the Blond Ambition Tour and The Girlie Show Tour.
She wore clothes of designers before they got famous such as Olivier Theyskens and Rick Owens, helping them to boost their careers. 
She plays with gender-bending fashion like no other did.

content:
Brenden Harris In my family happened that too. 
I come from a family of Portuguese immigrants who landed in Argentina during the Big Depression. You have no idea how hard it was to start from scratch and more with the disaster that was happening in the world. Perón and Evita were revolutionary, they made a ton of laws that nowadays we consider it “normal” but in that moment were just incredible, and more for the women, because they started to have a voice; as well as the workers, who started to be recognized as people and not slaves. 

The same immigrants that I told you before became fabric owners during the “peronismo”. Nowadays, my grandma is the only one who lives and still remembers the magic of that period and how his dad took his truck every time Evita or Perón talked in Plaza de Mayo, and took all the people of the neighborhood to there to see them talk. 
The ones who hated Perón were who were in disadvantage because of all the rights that the people were receiving; and nowadays, the ones who do it are basically ignorants.
I’m so happy that people who live abroad knows about this wonderful spirits,  and the story of your grandma is so inspiring, she’s such a strong woman, send her hugs! 
- María Eva (they named me in honor of evita haha🥰)

You're spot on—Madonna's "Die Another Day" absolutely felt like a sonic curveball when it dropped in 2002, blending that gritty electroclash vibe with orchestral swells in a way that didn't quite fit the mold of traditional Bond themes or mainstream pop at the time. 
 Its futuristic production, courtesy of Mirwais Ahmadzaï, pushed boundaries with distorted vocals, glitchy beats, and introspective lyrics about ego death and self-reinvention, which foreshadowed the rise of edgier electronic pop in the 2010s from artists like Lady Gaga or even the experimental edges of EDM. 
 No wonder it blew your mind on first listen; a lot of fans and critics retrospectively call it ahead of its curve by a decade or more, especially for how it subverted expectations in a Bond context. 
 That said, it wasn't universally loved back then—some slammed it as "noisy" or mismatched 
—but hindsight has been kinder, proving Madonna's knack for trendsetting once again. What's your favorite part of the track—the Freud reference, the build-up, or that killer video?

​ @lashercollette88  MJ was never creative, sorry. He was doing the same routine again and again, and again until he died. There are videos of him performing the moonwalk, with the same white socks, black hat,  red shirt. In terms of being CREATIVE, MJ could not compete with Madonna, he always took the road more traveled by, he took the moonwalk road and high pitch screams for 20 years, his fans super happy to expect that from him.

The video implies that the joyride was the grandmother's idea -- see her watching the car chase at 3:49 and responding like she wants to be in the car on the television, wanting to feel that for herself. The video also implies that Madonna's character is dying from some sort of terminal disease -- see the shot at 4:00 of all those pill bottles. So, since they both have only a little time left to live: on the grandma's suggestion, they both take this crazy joyride, doing all the transgressive violent things they've ever wanted to do, before going out with a bang. 

Yes, Madonna's character is also possibly fleeing the law (the multiple licenses) and some sort of abusive relationship (see the bruise at 3:53) but I feel like that's more about telling you what sort of person she is --  how she's been made into a victim and a target by her relationship and the law. So, for her, the joyride is also a last, wild grab at feeling powerful again before going out in a bang.

As for the shin guards and bulletproof vest: I feel like those are there mostly to let us know that they did plan this ahead of time, and they BOTH knew what was planned to happen that night -- the grandma as well as Madonna. Obviously they're not going to survive that crash. But also, putting on "armor" before getting into a car that you will ride to your death can feel like getting dressed for battle, in an invigorating and powerful way. So, you could understand the armor as meaning something symbolic to Madonna and the grandma, even if it won't literally protect them.

content:
The story being told in this video is very interesting.  The little boy waiting outside and not being allowed inside, he represents 'innocence' a purity that is untainted and uncorrupted, and the childlike wonder that we often lose as we grow older and go through life. 

It's possible he represents Madonnas innocence.  She is partaking in a peep show, dancing, performing for the benefit of men. They look upon her as nothing more than an object of desire, yet they are prevented from.actually interacting with her the way they would like, which is showcased by the frustration they display by banging on the walls of their cubicles.  The end of the video with Madonna going off with the little boy, it could be a symbolic representation of Madonna taking back her innocence, her sense of childlike wonder and playfulness, the fact that she emerges from the peep show wearing an outfit identical to the little boy's outfit is significant.
